- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

6	//! Backend discovery and building.
7	//!
8	//! Finds or builds `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` using this priority:
9	//!
10	//! 1. `CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var (explicit override)
11	//! 2. Project config (`.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml`)
12	//! 3. Local repo (detected by presence of `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda`)
13	//! 4. Cached `.so` at `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/librustc_codegen_cuda.so`,
14	//!    but only when it isn't older than the running `cargo-oxide` binary
15	//! 5. Auto-fetch from git and build (one-time, or after a stale-cache miss)
16	//!
17	//! ## Cache staleness (issue #49)
18	//!
19	//! `cargo install` always rewrites `~/.cargo/bin/cargo-oxide` on every
20	//! upgrade, bumping its mtime. The cached `.so` is only ever written by
21	//! step 5 below, so a binary newer than the cache is the canonical signal
22	//! that the user has just upgraded `cargo-oxide` and the cached backend
23	//! no longer matches the binary loading it. When step 4 detects that, we
24	//! drop both the cached `.so` *and* the cached source tree so that step 5
25	//! re-clones fresh and rebuilds, rather than rebuilding from a clone that
26	//! was taken whenever the user first installed.
27	//!
28	//! ## Cache staleness vs. source (backend source advances)
29	//!
30	//! The binary-mtime check above does not fire when the developer updates
31	//! the backend SOURCE (the `rustc-codegen-cuda` crate) but leaves the
32	//! `cargo-oxide` binary unchanged. In that case the cached `.so` is older
33	//! than the source it was built from, yet the binary check sees no upgrade
34	//! and the stale backend is silently reused. To catch this we also compare
35	//! the cached `.so` against the newest mtime of the backend source inputs
36	//! (the crate's `src/**` and `Cargo.toml`) found in the cached source tree.
37	//! When the source tree cannot be located we degrade gracefully to the
38	//! binary-only check rather than erroring.
39	//!
40	//! The two stale signals call for different recovery. A binary upgrade means
41	//! the cached source may no longer match the new binary, so we drop the
42	//! source tree and re-clone fresh (above). A source advance means the cached
43	//! source IS the newer truth, so we rebuild the `.so` from that existing
44	//! source in place; re-cloning would throw away the very source that
45	//! triggered the rebuild. Binary staleness takes precedence when both fire.
46	//!
47	//! ## Cache staleness vs. toolchain (the active rustc changes)
48	//!
49	//! The mtime checks above miss a toolchain swap: the cached `.so` is
50	//! dynamically linked against one specific `librustc_driver-<hash>.so`, but a
51	//! repo `rust-toolchain.toml` or a changed default nightly leaves the
52	//! `cargo-oxide` binary and the cached source untouched. The stale `.so` then
53	//! loads against the wrong driver and fails with a cryptic
54	//! `librustc_driver-<hash>.so: cannot open shared object file`. To catch this
55	//! we record the active toolchain fingerprint (`rustc -vV`) next to the cached
56	//! `.so` at build time and compare it on every lookup; a recorded fingerprint
57	//! that differs from the active toolchain forces a fresh re-clone and rebuild.
58	//! This check has the highest precedence, since a toolchain mismatch makes the
59	//! cached `.so` unloadable regardless of mtimes. A cache predating the
60	//! fingerprint file defers to the mtime checks (a `cargo-oxide` reinstall or
61	//! `rm -rf ~/.cargo/cuda-oxide` heals those).
62

69	pub fn find_workspace_root() -> Option<PathBuf> {
70	    let mut dir = std::env::current_dir().ok()?;
71	    loop {
72	        if dir.join("crates/rustc-codegen-cuda").is_dir() && dir.join("Cargo.toml").is_file() {
73	            return Some(dir);
74	        }
75	        if !dir.pop() {

77	        }

82	///
83	/// Discovery order:
84	/// 1. `CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var
85	/// 2. Project config (`.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml`)
86	/// 3. Local repo build (crates/rustc-codegen-cuda)
87	/// 4. Cached build at ~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/
88	/// 5. Auto-fetch + build from git
89	pub fn find_or_build_backend(workspace_root: &Path, configured_backend: Option<&Path>) -> PathBuf {
90	    // 1. Explicit override
91	    if let Ok(path) = std::env::var("CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND") {
92	        let p = PathBuf::from(&path);

94	            return p;
95	        }
96	        eprintln!(
97	            "Warning: CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND={} does not exist, falling back to auto-detection",
98	            path
99	        );
100	    }

4	//! # `reserved-oxide-symbols` — INTERNAL workspace crate
5	//!
6	//! Single source of truth for the mangled symbol prefixes that the
7	//! `#[kernel]` / `#[device]` proc macros emit and that the codegen
8	//! backend, MIR-lowering, and LLVM-export passes consume.
9	//!
10	//! ## Not a public API
11	//!
12	//! This crate is `publish = false` and exists only to keep the macro side
13	//! and the consumer side of the cuda-oxide naming contract in lockstep.
14	//! The constants, builders, and predicates exposed here may change
15	//! without notice between commits. External consumers should depend on
16	//! `cuda-host`, `cuda-device`, or `cuda-macros` instead.
17	//!
18	//! ## What this crate owns
19	//!
20	//! The `cuda_oxide_*` namespace, reserved for cuda-oxide internal symbols.
21	//! Every prefix below ends with `246e25db_`, which is
22	//! `sha256("cuda_oxide_ + rust")` truncated to 8 hex chars. The hash
23	//! makes accidental collisions effectively impossible — nobody writes
24	//! `fn cuda_oxide_kernel_246e25db_foo()` by accident.
25	//!
26	//! ## Layered API
27	//!
28	//! - **Constants** ([`KERNEL_PREFIX`] etc.) — the raw prefix strings.
29	//! - **Builders** ([`kernel_symbol`] etc.) — for the macro side.
30	//! - **Predicates and extractors** ([`is_kernel_symbol`],
31	//!   [`kernel_base_name`], etc.) — for the consumer side.
32	//!
33	//! The Layer-3 helpers hide the substring matching that used to be
34	//! duplicated across `rustc-codegen-cuda`, `llvm-export`, and `mir-lower`.
35	//!
36	//! ## Mutual exclusion guarantee
37	//!
38	//! [`DEVICE_PREFIX`] and [`DEVICE_EXTERN_PREFIX`] are mutually exclusive
39	//! substrings: a symbol containing one cannot contain the other. This is
40	//! a property of the hash suffix and is verified by a unit test below.
41	//! Consumers therefore do **not** need the historical
42	//! `contains(DEVICE_PREFIX) && !contains(DEVICE_EXTERN_PREFIX)` ordering
43	//! dance — [`is_device_symbol`] handles the disambiguation internally.
44	
45	#![no_std]
46	#![doc(hidden)]
47	
48	extern crate alloc;
49	
50	use alloc::format;
51	use alloc::string::String;
52	
53	// ============================================================================
54	// Layer 1 — raw constants
55	// ============================================================================
56	
57	/// Reserved root that prefixes every cuda-oxide internal symbol.
58	///
59	/// User code must not define functions whose name starts with this.
60	/// The `#[kernel]` and `#[device]` proc macros enforce this rule at
61	/// the source-code level by checking `name.starts_with(RESERVED_ROOT)`
62	/// and emitting a compile error.
63	pub const RESERVED_ROOT: &str = "cuda_oxide_";
64	
65	/// Magic suffix appended to every prefix to defend against accidental
66	/// name collisions in user code.
67	///
68	/// `sha256("cuda_oxide_ + rust")` truncated to 8 hex chars. The exact
69	/// value is irrelevant — what matters is that it's fixed forever and
70	/// no human would ever type it as part of a regular function name.
71	pub const HASH_SUFFIX: &str = "246e25db";
72	
73	/// Prefix added to `#[kernel]` functions for collector detection.
74	///
75	/// `#[kernel] fn vecadd(...)` becomes `fn cuda_oxide_kernel_246e25db_vecadd(...)`.
76	/// The collector finds these by name; the PTX entry name itself is the
77	/// unprefixed base (e.g., `vecadd`).
78	pub const KERNEL_PREFIX: &str = "cuda_oxide_kernel_246e25db_";
79	
80	/// Prefix added to `#[device]` functions for collector detection.
81	///
82	/// `#[device] fn helper(...)` becomes `fn cuda_oxide_device_246e25db_helper(...)`.
83	/// The LLVM-export layer strips this prefix to produce clean device-side
84	/// symbol names in the final PTX/LTOIR.
85	pub const DEVICE_PREFIX: &str = "cuda_oxide_device_246e25db_";
86	
87	/// Prefix added to functions inside `#[device] extern "C" { ... }` blocks.
88	///
89	/// `#[device] extern "C" { fn foo(); }` becomes
90	/// `fn cuda_oxide_device_extern_246e25db_foo();`. The MIR-lowering pass
91	/// strips this prefix when emitting the LLVM `declare` so that the LTOIR
92	/// linker resolves against the original (unprefixed) external symbol.
93	pub const DEVICE_EXTERN_PREFIX: &str = "cuda_oxide_device_extern_246e25db_";
94

97	///

# CUDA-Oxide Examples Catalog & Build Tooling Map

## Task 1: Key Example Directories and Kernel Concepts

**Total examples found:** 118 example directories under `/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/`

### Requested KEY Examples with Kernel Concept Descriptions:

1. **vecadd** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/vecadd/`)
   - **Concept:** Demonstrates unified vector addition with single-file compilation, where `#[cuda_module]` generates a module containing a `#[kernel]` function that gets compiled to PTX by rustc-codegen-cuda while host code compiles via standard LLVM. The final binary contains both host code and embedded PTX.

2. **host_closure** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/host_closure/`)
   - **Concept:** Tests the typed `#[cuda_module]` launch path with generic kernels taking closures, exercising the kernel-boundary ABI fix that passes closures as single byval `.param` structs rather than flattened per-capture, supporting `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce` trait bounds with multiple capture patterns.

3. **generic** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/generic/`)
   - **Concept:** Validates that the collector correctly handles monomorphized generic kernels, testing whether generic definitions like `fn scale<T>(factor: T, ...)` generate separate PTX entry points when monomorphized (e.g., `scale::<f32>` and `scale::<i32>`).

4. **atomics** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/atomics/`)
   - **Concept:** Comprehensive test suite for sound atomic operations across phases covering DeviceAtomicU32/I32 with load/store/fetch_add/CAS at various memory orderings (Relaxed, Acquire, Release, AcqRel, SeqCst), then expands to 64-bit atomics, additional RMW ops (fetch_sub, bitwise, swap, min/max), floating-point atomics, and multi-scope operations (Block, Device, System).

5. **atomic_f16** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/atomic_f16/`)
   - **Concept:** Half-precision atomic histogram checks demonstrating f16 atomic operations (DeviceAtomicF16, BlockAtomicF16, SystemAtomicF16) with fetch_add, testing both device-scope and block-scope histogram accumulation with relaxed ordering.

6. **barrier** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/barrier/`)
   - **Concept:** Demonstrates mbarrier (async barrier) intrinsics including `mbarrier_init`, `mbarrier_arrive`, `mbarrier_test_wait`, and `mbarrier_inval` for thread synchronization, showing static barrier initialization in shared memory and non-blocking phase completion checks.

7. **cluster** (`/home/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cluster/`)
   - **Concept:** Hopper cluster intrinsic tests (sm_90+) demonstrating cluster special registers (`cluster_ctaidX`, `cluster_nctaidX`, etc.), cluster synchronization (`cluster_sync`), distributed shared memory (`map_shared_rank`), and compile-time cluster configuration via `#[cluster_launch(x,y,z)]` attribute.

8. **async_mlp** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/async_mlp/`)
   - **Concept:** Full `cuda-async` execution model demonstration with multi-kernel forward pass (GEMM → MatVec → ReLU) showcasing async patterns: `with_context` for stream-aware memory operations as `DeviceOperations`, `and_then` for chaining dependent launches, `zip!` for combining independent allocations, `.arc()` for sharing weights across batches, and `tokio::spawn` for concurrent batch execution.

9. **async_vecadd** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/async_vecadd/`)
   - **Concept:** Simplified async vector addition demonstrating `#[cuda_module]` generating typed async launch methods where `vecadd_async` returns a lazy `DeviceOperation`, `.await` schedules on a round-robin stream pool, `.sync()` blocks the calling thread, and `and_then`/`zip!` provide operation chaining.

10. **tcgen05** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/tcgen05/`)
    - **Concept:** Blackwell-only (sm_100/sm_120) Tensor Core Gen 5 infrastructure testing `cta_group::1` (single CTA) and `cta_group::2` (CTA pairs on adjacent SMs) operations including tcgen05_alloc/dealloc, tcgen05_fence_before/after_thread_sync, tcgen05_commit, tcgen05_mma_ws_f16, and tcgen05_cp_smem_to_tmem with instruction/descriptor builders.

11. **gemm_sol_final** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/gemm_sol_final/`)
    - **Concept:** Canonical Blackwell GEMM speed-of-light example with resource-specialized entry points (M256xN256 for 4096/8192 and M512xN256 for 16384) sharing CLC + `cta_group::2` design, four-stage shared-memory pipeline, compiler-directed K-loop unrolling, L2-aware output-tile ordering, and vectorized 64-bit epilogue stores on f16/bf16 data.

12. **clc** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/clc/`)
    - **Concept:** Cluster Launch Control (CLC) test for Blackwell SM 100+ exercising all 6 CLC intrinsics (`clc_try_cancel`/`clc_try_cancel_multicast` for async work-stealing, `clc_query_is_canceled`, `clc_query_get_first_ctaid_{x,y,z}`) to verify running CTAs can steal pending CTAs' tile coordinates and write them to global memory.

13. **bf16x2_fma** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/bf16x2_fma/`)
    - **Concept:** Behavior example for the packed bf16x2 FMA intrinsic, demonstrating conversion from f32x2 to bf16x2 pairs using `cvt_f32x2_bf16x2`, performing fused multiply-add on packed representations, and packing/unpacking bf16 values into u32 for storage.

14. **device_ffi_test** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/device_ffi_test/`)
    - **Concept:** Demonstrates calling device functions defined in external LTOIR (compiled from CUDA C++) from cuda-oxide kernels via `#[device] extern "C"` blocks, testing functions like `magnitude_squared`, `fast_rsqrt`, `dot_product`, and `warp_reduce_sum` without requiring NVVM attributes since the external LTOIR already contains proper attributes.

15. **cross_crate_kernel** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cross_crate_kernel/`)
    - **Concept:** Tests using kernels defined in a library crate, where generic kernels like `kernel_lib::scale<T>` are monomorphized at the use site in the consuming binary, PTX is generated during binary compilation, and const-generic entries are instantiated for multiple values across crate boundaries.

16. **cuda_module_in_lib** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cuda_module_in_lib/`)
    - **Concept:** Regression test for issue #72 demonstrating `#[cuda_module]` in a library crate where concrete kernels have their PTX embedded as an `.oxart` data section in the library's `.rlib`, with link-anchor symbols ensuring the artifact member reaches the final binary and `kernels::load(&ctx)` succeeds at runtime.

17. **cutile_inter_kernel** (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/cutile_inter_kernel/`)
    - **Concept:** Host/tile side of cuda-oxide <> cutile-rs interop demonstrating a pipeline where cutile-rs Tile kernel computes row-wise softmax, cuda-oxide SIMT kernel thresholds and scales the result, both running on the same CUDA stream and sharing device tensors, showcasing async execution with `DeviceFuture` and `ExecutionContext`.

### crates/cargo-oxide/src/commands.rs (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/cargo-oxide/src/commands.rs`)

**Summary:** Backend discovery and building logic finding or building `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` with priority order: (1) `CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var override, (2) Project config `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml`, (3) Local repo `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda`, (4) Cached `.so` at `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/` with staleness checks against binary mtime, source mtime, and toolchain fingerprint, (5) Auto-fetch from git and build. Includes `find_workspace_root()` for workspace detection by walking up from CWD.

### Justfile (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/Justfile`)
**Summary:** Just recipes for cuda-oxide development workflow: `fmt`/`fmt-check` (format via cargo oxide fmt), `clippy`/`clippy-fix`, `test` (unit tests excluding rustc_private/device-only), `doc-check` (docs + doctests), `check` (all checks), `clean-artifacts`, `build <example>`, `run <example>`, `pipeline <example>`, `smoketest` (GPU-aware gating via scripts/smoketest.sh), `check-errors` (verify error* examples in STATUS.md).

### crates/oxide-artifacts/src/ (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/oxide-artifacts/src/`)
**Files:** `lib.rs` only
**Description:** Defines the embedded device artifact bundle format (.oxart section), a backend-independent wire format for generated device code. The bundle includes magic header ("OXIDEART"), version, producer name, device target, and payload records (PTX, NVVM IR, LTOIR, CUBIN) with entry records (kernels, device functions). Uses ArtifactPayloadSpec/ArtifactEntrySpec for building bundles and `.oxlink` section anchor symbols to prevent linker stripping.

### crates/reserved-oxide-symbols/src/ (`/home/runner/work/codeReader/codeReader/work/NVlabs-cuda-oxide/crates/reserved-oxide-symbols/src/`)
**Files:** `lib.rs` only
**Description:** Single source of truth for cuda-oxide's mangled symbol prefix contract between `#[kernel]`/`#[device]` proc macros and the codegen backend. Defines constants (KERNEL_PREFIX, DEVICE_PREFIX, DEVICE_EXTERN_PREFIX, HASH_SUFFIX) all ending in `246e25db_` (sha256 hash). Provides layered API: raw constants, builders (kernel_symbol, device_symbol, etc.) for macros, and predicates/extractors (is_kernel_symbol, is_device_symbol, kernel_base_name, etc.) for consumers, ensuring mutual exclusion between device prefixes.
